design_philosophy.core_loop: Select wrestler with unique moveset → engage in one-on-one or tag-team wrestling matches → defeat opponent via pinfall or submission → progress through championship ladder → defend belt against full roster
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Risk/reward of grappling vs striking; timing opponent interactions to execute special moves and finishers before being countered
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Become a professional wrestler champion by mastering a character's signature techniques and defeating increasingly skilled opponents
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Hybrid fighting game using wrestling mechanics rather than pure combat; emphasizes grappling, holds, and submission victories over health depletion; character roster spans multiple international wrestling archetypes (Russian, Mexican luchador, Japanese kabuki-themed, etc.), treating wrestling as cultural performance art

difficulty.continues_system: Continues system present; Grater's ending content differs based on completion without continues
difficulty.checkpointing: Match-based progression; defeated opponents do not respawn within single championship run
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Character selection provides difficulty variance—different wrestlers have distinct movesets and difficulty curves; two game modes (Single Match vs Team Battle Royale) offer different progression paths

progression.unlock_mechanism: Jumbo Flapjack and Scorpion are non-playable boss characters in Single Match mode but become selectable in Team Battle Royale; championship defense structure unlocks after initial roster victory
replayability_hooks: Ten character roster with distinct movesets encouraging multiple playthroughs
Single Match mode vs Team Battle Royale provide different progression experiences
Up to four-player multiplayer support incentivizes repeated competitive sessions
Character-specific endings and victory quotes create narrative variation
Completion without continues unlocks alternate ending content

world_building_techniques: Character introductions via selection screen descriptions and backstories
Inter-character relationships implied through dialogue (Zalazof/Gunloc rivalry, Haggar's apprentices, Stingray/Oni antagonism)
Localization changes: English version removed Japanese apprenticeship structure but maintained Street Fighter universe connections (Guile/Gunloc kinship, Zangief reference, Haggar as former Mayor)
Victory and loss quotes provide personality and context to character conflicts
Jessica (from Final Fight) appears to celebrate Haggar's victories, establishing cross-series continuity

boss_design.philosophy: Boss wrestlers represent distinct fighting archetypes and escalating challenge; final opponents (Jumbo Flapjack, Scorpion) are non-playable to emphasize their special status within championship structure
notable_bosses: Jumbo Flapjack (Kimala the Bouncer) – Exceptionally large and sadistic boss character
Scorpion – Non-playable boss in Single Match, selectable in Team Battle Royale
Championship roster progression suggests increasing wrestler skill levels

faction_indicators: International wrestler archetypes suggest regional/cultural affiliations: Russian (Biff/Zalazof), Mexican (El Stingray), Japanese (Great Oni), English (Titanic Tim), Australian (Alexander the Grater), Jamaican (King Rasta Mon)
Trainer-student hierarchies in Japanese narrative (Haggar as mentor to Zalazof and Colt)
Tag team partnerships established through character histories (Titanic Tim with Street Fighter's Birdie)
danger_signals: Sadistic wrestling style emphasized for Jumbo Flapjack and Alexander the Grater
King Rasta Mon characterized as 'wild and feral-like' with 'savage beast' combat behavior—culturally reductive stereotype
Physical dominance and intimidation as narrative justification for strength (Titanic Tim uses 'size and strength to intimidate')
environmental_storytelling: Wrestling ring serves as cultural performance space where international styles and personalities clash
Championship belt acquisition and defense structure mirrors real professional wrestling tournament hierarchies
Character designs by Tetsuo Hara (Fist of the North Star artist) treat wrestlers as manga archetypes with visual storytelling through costume and silhouette

sources_used: tcrf (The Cutting Room Floor) - Saturday Night Slam Masters (SNES) article
wikipedia - Saturday Night Slam Masters article
